title: Loot boxes / gacha
evidenceNote: Replicated, meta-analytic correlation with problem gambling; effect stronger in adolescents.
summary: Paid, randomised reward containers whose contents — and often whose odds — are unknown before purchase.
howItWorks: Players spend real money (often via an intermediate premium currency) for a randomised draw. Variable-ratio reinforcement and near-miss framing encourage repeat purchases, and 'pity' counters extend the spend cycle.
whyHarmful: The structure is psychologically akin to gambling and shows a robust, replicated correlation with problem gambling — strongest among adolescents — with revenue concentrated in a small share of high-spenders. It is the clearest case of exploitation that persists even when fully transparent.
examples: Gacha 'banners' in mobile RPGs with rate-up characters
Card or skin packs bought with premium currency
Sports-title player packs in 'ultimate team' modes

title: Hidden / undisclosed odds
evidenceNote: Central to gambling-regulation analyses; disclosure is a leading policy remedy.
summary: The probability of outcomes in a paid randomised reward is withheld, buried, or hard to verify.
howItWorks: Drop rates are omitted, hidden in submenus, or stated in ways players cannot verify, so the true expected value of a purchase is unknowable at the point of sale.
whyHarmful: Without odds, players cannot form an accurate belief about what their money buys — a textbook deceptive failure of material transparency. Mandatory odds disclosure is among the most widely proposed regulatory fixes.
examples: A loot box that lists items but not their drop rates
Odds shown only in an external web page, not in the purchase flow

title: Premium-currency obfuscation
evidenceNote: Documented in player-perception and consumer-law analyses of mobile monetization.
summary: Real money is converted into in-game currency at non-round ratios that break the player's price intuition.
howItWorks: Purchases are denominated in gems/coins sold in bundles that never quite match item prices, leaving stranded balances and obscuring the real cost of any single item.
whyHarmful: The extra conversion step severs the felt link between money and goods, so players systematically under-perceive what they are spending — especially harmful for children and when combined with randomised rewards.
examples: Buying 1,000 gems for a 1,200-gem item, forcing another purchase
Multiple stacked currencies (coins, gems, tickets)

title: Bundle anchoring & decoys
evidenceNote: Consistent with player-reported pricing tactics; grounded in choice-architecture research.
summary: Tiered packs are priced to steer players toward a 'best value' middle option through anchoring and decoys.
howItWorks: An overpriced top tier and a token bottom tier make the target mid-tier look rational, while leftover balances nudge the next purchase.
whyHarmful: The arrangement exploits anchoring and the decoy effect to shift spending upward without adding real value, privileging the provider's interest over the player's.
examples: Starter / Value / Mega coin packs where 'Value' is the obvious pick
Limited 'bundle' deals that bundle in unwanted items

title: Pay-to-win
evidenceNote: Strong, consistent player-perceived unfairness in competitive contexts.
summary: Purchasable power converts money into competitive advantage, undermining the implicit contract of skill.
howItWorks: Players can buy items, stats, or progression that materially affect competitive outcomes against non-paying players.
whyHarmful: It breaks the fairness expectation at the heart of competitive play; players consistently rate it among the most unfair monetization practices, and it pressures spending to stay competitive.
examples: Buyable best-in-slot gear in competitive PvP
Paid stat boosts that decide ranked matches

title: Pay-to-skip / engineered grind
evidenceNote: Documented in exploitation-focused analyses of monetized game services.
summary: Progression is deliberately slowed so the game can sell time-savers that remove the friction it introduced.
howItWorks: Designers tune progression to be tedious, then offer paid shortcuts; the 'problem' and the 'solution' are both manufactured.
whyHarmful: It converts the player's time into a lever for extraction and shrinks the realistic free path, shading from manipulation into coercion when progression is effectively withheld until payment.
examples: Doubled XP or instant-build offers after a deliberately slow curve
Resource caps that make the free path impractical

title: FOMO / limited-time offers
evidenceNote: Recurring in player reports and early-childhood gaming studies.
summary: Artificial scarcity and urgency pressure players into purchases before a countdown expires.
howItWorks: Limited-time banners, expiring bundles, and countdown timers manufacture a fear of missing out that shortcuts deliberation.
whyHarmful: Time pressure at the decision point interferes with reasoning and pushes impulsive spending; data-triggered offers can target moments of heightened susceptibility.
examples: 'Only today!' starter bundles for new players
Rotating limited banners tied to events

title: Monetising basic quality-of-life
evidenceNote: Reported by players as an unfair, friction-for-sale tactic.
summary: The game charges to remove friction that the game itself introduced.
howItWorks: Basic conveniences — extra inventory, auto-collect, faster menus — are withheld and sold back as upgrades.
whyHarmful: It manufactures inconvenience to extract payment for relief, an asymmetric transfer of value that benefits the provider at the player's expense.
examples: Paid inventory expansions in games with deliberately tiny default storage
Selling an 'auto-play' for a grindy loop

title: Bait-and-switch / product not as expected
evidenceNote: Frequent in player reviews of mobile monetization.
summary: The advertised content or experience differs materially from what is actually delivered.
howItWorks: Ads or store pages depict gameplay, characters, or value that the installed product does not contain.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about what is being bought, the core of deception; players report frustration and a sense of being misled.
examples: Ads showing a puzzle minigame that barely appears in the real game
Store screenshots not representative of actual play

title: Predatory / forced advertising
evidenceNote: Prevalent in children's apps per reliable content-coding studies.
summary: Unskippable or rewarded ads — sometimes disguised as content — are bundled into progression.
howItWorks: Players must watch ads to continue or to claim rewards, and ads are often styled to look like gameplay, especially in children's titles.
whyHarmful: It forces attention and, when disguised, deceives — disproportionately reaching children who cannot reliably distinguish ads from play.
examples: Mandatory ad before each level retry
An 'ad' that mimics the game's own UI

title: Accidental-purchase / default-to-purchase UI
evidenceNote: Documented in 3D/UGC player-perception work and consumer-protection enforcement.
summary: Purchase is the default or easily mis-tapped path, so spending happens without express, informed consent.
howItWorks: Buy buttons sit where 'continue' usually is, confirmation is minimal, and stored payment makes a tap a charge.
whyHarmful: It manufactures purchases the player did not intend — a consent failure that has drawn major regulatory action, and is acute for children.
examples: A 'claim reward' button that actually buys currency
One-tap purchases with no meaningful confirmation

title: Subscription / battle-pass traps
evidenceNote: Analysed in free-to-play transition research.
summary: Easy entry and obstructed cancellation, with 'earned' value that expires if you stop paying or playing.
howItWorks: Battle passes and subscriptions create loss-averse commitment: progress and rewards are time-boxed, and cancelling is harder than subscribing.
whyHarmful: It withholds and penalises exit, the coercive core, converting fear of wasted investment into recurring spend and obligatory play.
examples: A season pass whose unspent tiers vanish at reset
Subscriptions easy to start but buried to cancel

title: Grinding / engineered repetition
evidenceNote: Often normalised as 'progression'; harmful chiefly when monetized.
summary: Repetitive effort is inflated to extend play or to drive pay-to-skip purchases.
howItWorks: Reward curves are tuned so meaningful progress requires large amounts of repetitive activity.
whyHarmful: Benign as pacing, it becomes exploitative when paired with paid shortcuts or when the dose is tuned for retention over enjoyment. A clear case where context, not the mechanic, decides harm.
examples: Hundreds of identical runs for one upgrade
XP curves that flatten right before a paywall

title: Energy / wait timers (appointment mechanics)
evidenceNote: Classic temporal dark pattern; documented in early-childhood gaming.
summary: Play is gated by real-time cooldowns that can be bypassed for money.
howItWorks: An energy/lives meter depletes and refills on a real-world clock, prompting players to return on the game's schedule or pay to continue.
whyHarmful: It colonises the player's real time and converts impatience into spend; appointment scheduling is especially hard on children who lack control over when they play.
examples: Five 'lives' that refill one per 30 minutes
Timed building/crafting that costs gems to finish now

title: Daily login / streaks
evidenceNote: Loss-aversion mechanic noted across temporal-pattern and child-app studies.
summary: Escalating rewards and loss-aversion penalties punish missing a day.
howItWorks: Consecutive-day rewards grow, and breaking the streak resets them — turning a habit into an obligation.
whyHarmful: It manufactures anxiety about missing out and binds daily attention through loss aversion rather than genuine enjoyment.
examples: A 30-day reward calendar that resets on a single miss
Streak counters with escalating bonuses

title: Time fog / playtime opacity
evidenceNote: Part of the newer attention-capture deceptive-design literature.
summary: Elapsed time and session length are concealed so players lose track of how long they have played.
howItWorks: The interface omits clocks, session timers, or stopping cues, keeping the player in a timeless flow.
whyHarmful: Hiding time obscures a material fact about the cost of play, degrading the player's ability to self-regulate attention.
examples: No in-game clock or session timer
Seamless level chaining with no natural stop

title: Interruptible-but-sticky loops
evidenceNote: Attention-capture mechanics; evidence base still developing.
summary: Auto-advancing or endless content resists natural stopping points.
howItWorks: The next round, reward, or item loads automatically, removing the friction that would prompt a player to stop.
whyHarmful: By engineering away stopping cues it captures attention beyond the player's intention, a temporal harm to digital wellbeing.
examples: Auto-queue into the next match
Continuous reward feeds with no end screen

title: Social obligation / guilt
evidenceNote: Reported across UGC and competitive-fairness studies.
summary: The design leverages teammates' dependence to compel continued play or spending.
howItWorks: Shared goals, guild quotas, or co-op stakes make stepping away feel like letting others down.
whyHarmful: It applies interpersonal pressure that overrides the player's own preference about when to stop, shading into coercion through social cost.
examples: Guild contribution quotas with penalties
Co-op events where dropping out hurts friends

title: Parasocial-character pressure
evidenceNote: Documented as a prevalent manipulative tactic in children's apps.
summary: Beloved characters urge purchases or continued play, exploiting parasocial attachment.
howItWorks: A trusted mascot pleads, looks sad, or rewards the player for spending, leveraging the emotional bond — especially powerful with children.
whyHarmful: It exploits a developmental vulnerability: children attribute feelings and authority to characters and cannot easily resist their 'requests'.
examples: A sad mascot asking the child not to leave
A character who 'gives a gift' that opens the store

title: Gifting / invitation spam (social pyramid)
evidenceNote: Long-noted virality tactic; systematic game-specific evidence still thin.
summary: Progress is tied to recruiting or pestering friends.
howItWorks: Rewards or gating require inviting contacts, sharing, or gifting, turning the player into a distribution channel.
whyHarmful: It instrumentalises relationships and can harvest contact data, transferring social capital and privacy to the provider.
examples: 'Invite 3 friends to unlock'
Daily gift-spam to a contact list

title: Impersonation / disguised system-as-friend
evidenceNote: Reported in 3D/UGC player-perception research as interpersonal persuasion.
summary: System messages are styled to look like communication from a peer.
howItWorks: Automated prompts are framed as if a friend or another player sent them, borrowing social trust.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about who is speaking, using manufactured social proof to steer choices the player would otherwise resist.
examples: A 'message' from a fake teammate urging a purchase
NPC framed as a real co-player

title: Monetised social status
evidenceNote: Linked to gamblification and competitive-fairness perceptions.
summary: Visible status is sold to exploit peer comparison and the desire to belong.
howItWorks: Cosmetics, badges, or ranks that signal spending are made visible to others, turning social comparison into a sales driver.
whyHarmful: It monetises belonging and esteem, pressuring spend through peer comparison rather than intrinsic value — acute among younger players.
examples: Conspicuous paid skins that mark non-payers
Leaderboards that reward spending visibly

title: Variable-ratio reward / near-miss
evidenceNote: Core gambling psychology; well-evidenced reinforcement mechanism.
summary: Slot-machine-like reinforcement and 'almost won' framing drive repeated attempts.
howItWorks: Rewards arrive on an unpredictable schedule, and near-miss outcomes are highlighted to imply success was close.
whyHarmful: Variable-ratio schedules are the most persistent form of reinforcement, and near-miss framing fuels chasing — the engine behind randomised-reward harm.
examples: A pull that lands just short of the rare item
Reels or spins that frequently show near-wins

title: Sunk-cost / entrapment
evidenceNote: Mechanism described across predatory-monetization analyses.
summary: Designs make prior investment justify further spending or time.
howItWorks: Progress, collections, or partial purchases are framed so quitting feels like wasting what is already invested.
whyHarmful: It exploits the sunk-cost fallacy to keep players committing more money and time against their own interest.
examples: 'You're 80% to the prize — keep going'
Collections that feel wasteful to abandon

title: Illusion of control / skill framing
evidenceNote: Identified in exploitation analyses of chance-based mechanics.
summary: Chance outcomes are presented as if skill or choice could influence them.
howItWorks: Stop buttons, 'pick a box', or timing flourishes imply agency over results that are actually random.
whyHarmful: It fosters a false belief that effort or skill changes the odds, encouraging continued play and spend on what is pure chance.
examples: A 'tap to stop' wheel whose result is predetermined
Choose-your-box reveals over fixed odds

title: Aesthetic / sensory manipulation
evidenceNote: Reinforcing 'celebration' effects noted in gambling-adjacent design.
summary: Celebratory audio-visual feedback reinforces spending and reward moments.
howItWorks: Lights, fanfares, and animations amplify every reward — sometimes even net-loss outcomes — to feel like a win.
whyHarmful: Sensory reinforcement strengthens the spend-reward loop and can disguise losses as wins, nudging continued play.
examples: Big celebratory effects for a low-value pull
Fanfare on a 'win' that cost more than it returned

title: Aggressive nagging / interruptive upsell
evidenceNote: Nagging is a well-established cross-domain dark-pattern family.
summary: Frequent pop-ups repeatedly steer the player to the store.
howItWorks: Interruptive prompts surface offers on launch, between levels, and at idle moments, wearing down resistance.
whyHarmful: Repetition imposes an attention tax and pressures purchases through persistence rather than value.
examples: A store pop-up every time you open the app
Offers interrupting the flow between levels

title: Hidden costs
evidenceNote: Canonical dark pattern carried into games.
summary: The true cost of a purchase is revealed late, or never.
howItWorks: Additional charges, currency conversions, or requirements appear only after the player is committed.
whyHarmful: Withholding a material fact about price deceives the player and undermines informed consent.
examples: A 'free' item that needs paid currency to use
Costs that surface only at the final step

title: Confirmshaming
evidenceNote: Well-documented interface-interference pattern.
summary: Opt-out wording is laden with guilt to discourage the protective choice.
howItWorks: The decline button is phrased to make saying no feel foolish or shameful ('No thanks, I hate saving money').
whyHarmful: It manipulates through emotion rather than reasons, eroding autonomy at the point of choice.
examples: 'No, I don't want to get stronger' to decline an offer
Guilt-worded unsubscribe prompts

title: Misdirection / false visual hierarchy
evidenceNote: Recognised across mobile-app dark-pattern studies.
summary: Visual salience steers the player toward the provider-preferred option.
howItWorks: Color, size, and placement make the spend or opt-in button dominant while the alternative is muted.
whyHarmful: It biases choices by exploiting attention rather than informing it, quietly redirecting decisions.
examples: A bright 'Buy' button beside a greyed-out 'No'
Pre-highlighted most-expensive option

title: Obstructed exit / cancellation (sludge)
evidenceNote: Sludge / cancellation friction documented in gambling and account-closure studies.
summary: Asymmetric friction makes quitting, refunding, or disabling far harder than starting.
howItWorks: Cancellation is buried, multi-step, or routed through obstacles, while signup is one tap.
whyHarmful: It penalises and withholds exit — the coercive core — trapping spend and time and overriding the player's choice to leave.
examples: Refund links hidden deep in settings
Cancellation that demands multiple confirmations and waits

title: Disguised ads / content
evidenceNote: Prevalent in children's apps per reliable content analysis.
summary: Ads are styled as gameplay or rewards so the player cannot tell promotion from play.
howItWorks: Advertising is camouflaged within the game's own visual language, blurring the line between content and commerce.
whyHarmful: It deceives about what is an advertisement — especially harmful for children, who struggle to recognise persuasive intent.
examples: A 'reward' button that opens an ad/store
Ad banners drawn to look like collectible items

title: Value obfuscation / false narratives
evidenceNote: Reported in consumer-law and player-perception work on monetization.
summary: What a purchase actually yields is misrepresented through framing or false comparison.
howItWorks: Inflated 'original' prices, fake discounts, or vague yields make purchases look more valuable than they are.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about value, distorting the player's cost-benefit judgement at the point of sale.
examples: '80% off' against a price never charged
Bundles whose stated value double-counts items
